distributor.name: Center for Digital Research in the Humanities

availability.p: This edition is licensed under a Creative Commons Licence (available at http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/). You are free: to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work, and to make derivative works under the following conditions: you must attribute the work in the manner specified by the licensor; you may not use this work for commercial purposes; if you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you may distribute the resulting work only under a license identical to this one. For any reuse or distribution, you must make clear to others the license terms of this work. Any of these conditions can be waived if you get permission from the licensor. Your fair use and other rights are in no way affected by the above.

div.head: LECTURE X.

head.hi: And he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him.

div.p: The words which I have now recited will be sufficient to call your attention to a most awful, extraordinary, and interesting interview between God and man. The chapter, of which it forms nearly the commencement, and on the whole of which it is my purpose now to comment, must excite the attention of all who have ears to hear, however often it be repeated. The information which it contains as a moral, historic, and prophetic revelation, will abundantly repay our deepest research. It is indeed one of those links which so connect the declarations and dispensations of the Old Testament with those of the New, that they cannot be severed by the most industrious malice of man; and those who attempt the ungrateful and unprofitable task will find themselves fast bound in misery and contradition.

"And he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him."Genesis 18:2—He who life up his eyes was Abraham, whose faith and works obtained for him, among other illustrious titles that of "The friend of God." the three Beings who stood by him were of a divine nature. One of them, as the subsequent context informs us, was the Source of all power and might; He by whom all things were made, our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ; who afterwards, when incarnate, so signficantly told the Pharisees, "Verily Abraham saw my day, and rejoiced."John 8:56—The other two were of that class which is so sublimely described by the Psalmist, when he says, that the Lord maketh his angels spirits, and his ministers a flame of fire. The manner in which these three personages were first discerend by Abraham is characteristic of their quality. "He life up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him."Genesis 18:2 Though sitting in his tent, from which he could not but have commanded a view of all approaching objects, yet he seems to have been taken by surprise. The suddenness with which his guests manifested themselves to the patriarch is easy enough to be understood, when we consider their spirituality. How consistent are all teh records of inspiration! how unpardonable to overlook these minute testimonies of its truth! It was in fact a revelation above the laws of
nature; and it is plain that Abraham instantaneously acted under the impression, perahps the involuntary impression, of its being a special communication from Heaven. Great as are the prescriptive rights of hospitality, greater as they were in those ages than it is necessary they should now be, yet we ought to observe, that in Abraham's first address to those who so suddenly stood before him, there was a humility of deportment, and a sanctity of expression, with which man would not salute man, especially in the simple integrity of pastoral manners: he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself toward the ground, and said, "My Lord, if now I have found favour in they sight, pass not away, I pray thee, from they servant."Genesis 18:3 We shall therefore, probably, be nearest to the truth if we consider this revleation of the Divinity as confined to Abraham, and not necessarily extended to those who formed his household; and then we must be struck with the strict propriety of Abraham's conduct. It was not right for him to blazon what God thought fit to conceal, nor to remove that veil in which the Lord was pleased to shroud his glorious majesty. Abraham, therefore, treated with the best rites of human hospitality his Lord and his God, so long as the latter vouchsafed to retain a human appearance.

In the conduct of Abraham there is matter, not only for praise, but imitation. Does the Lord never visit us, when we least expect? Does he not visit us with afflictions, to try our faith; and blessings, to prove our gratitude? Do we acknowledge with alacrity and devotion the will and the power of the Lord, who revealeth not his presence? Moreover, when he comes to visit us as a spiritual Comforter, agreeably to the promise of the Lord Jesus Christ, desirous of remaining with us, do we welcome him as Abraham did Jehovah? Do we call him Lord? Do we ask him to turn in and tarry in our hearts? Do we kill and make ready? Do we kill and mortify our favourite sins? Do we set our house in order, and pray him to abide with us as a Comforter? My friends! the Holy Spirit is much more apt to knock at the door of our hearts, than we to rise and open unto him. Why are we thus slow to listen, and reluctant to admit the heavenly guest?—Because, when he speaketh of righteousness and judgment, like Felix, we tremble: therefore, like the foolish Roman, we say in our heart, I will hear thee at a more convenient time.

But Abraham loved the communion of his Maker, and great was his reward. To him were made known the future designs of a merciful and avenging God. The almighty had
two movtives in this manifestation of himself: 1st, To renew, for the sixth time, his promise of an heir, a son by Sarah his wife. The value and importance which God intended should be attached to this promise by man, is certainly to be argued from the frequency of its repitition. The value of it truly now is known to us, for we have the completion of it made manifest in the Gospel. But let us not, therefore, overlook, the origin of the hope that is in us; let us not count it a small thing, that God did SIX TIMES repeat to Abraham the assurance of a future Saviour. Let us not rashly denominate as merely the History of the Jews, THAT volume in which is recorded the exceeding great love of the Creator towards his creatures, and but for which we could now build up no claim on the faithfulness of God. Had there been no Old Testament, there could, in no sense, be a New one. Beward, then, ye who would say, Abba, Father! beware how ye interrupt the entail of your redemption! If your God be not the God of Israel, the promise of redemption is not unto you.

2dly, The Lord was moved to make known to Abraham the outgoings of his wrath against Sodom and Gomorrah. (Ver. 16.) "And the men rose up from thence and looked towards Sodom; and Abraham went with them, to
bring them on their way."Genesis 18:16 (Ver. 17.) "And the Lord said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do?"Genesis 18:17 Accordingly, the Almighty was pleased to inform Abraham, that the punishment about to fall on Sodom and Gomorrah was due to the enormous guilt of those places; that there was no excuse left for them; that he, the Lord, was come down to aascertain their iniquity (which is fully set forth in the subsequent chapter). (Ver 22.) "The men, therefore, turned their faces from thence, and went towards Sodom. But Abraham stood yet before the Lord."Genesis 18:22 By this we understand that the two angels in human form were sent to execute the wrath of the Lord on the ungodly, but that He, whose ministers they were, remained for a while with Abraham; to leave us a most astonishing instance of his long-suffering, and to prove that he is the God who in his justice remembereth mercy.

From the 23d verse to the end of the chapter, I would most willingly repeat the very words of Scripture, did time allow. Those who have time and means, cannot do better than peruse them this evening. It will make them sensible of God's abundant dispostion to pardon, and of their own hopeless condition without some intercession of a purer Being than themselves. Abraham most submissively entreats
the Judge of all the earth to pardon these devoted cities, for the sake of the righteous which peradventure might be among them. Doubtless, his heart has wrung with anguish for the fate of his nephew, Lot. Would the Almighty be entreated for the sake of fifty, of forty-five, of forty, of thirty, of twenty, of ten righteous? Ten righteous men would have saved Sodom; but Sodom and Gomorrah were not delivered.—What an admonition is here held out to us, that we should heed unto our ways; that we should endeavour to be of that number, for whose sake a little respite may be shown to the Canaanites of these times, ere an avenging God tread the winepress of his wrath! In such a hope there is no improper enthusiasm; our God is the same yesterday, to-day, and for ever. As evil communication corrupts good manners, so doth the fervant prayer of the righteous avail much. But prayer must be made unto One who is mighty to save; to Him on whose shoulder the government is laid—not to the inefficacious mediation of those, some of whom kept not their own estate; still less to those who, when alive, were of like passions with ourselves, and who, even in their ministry, feared lest themselves should be cast away. But, as there is one faith and one baptism, so is there one Intercessor, the man Jesus christ, who was before all worlds, and who hath showed us, by his
communion with Abraham, that he loveth to be entreated.

Were it necessary to set forth any further connecting points between the above portion of the Old Testament and the New, I should refer you to the Prophet Isaiah, who, though living under the law of Moses, is not improperly denominated the Evangelic Prophet, because so great a part of his mission is confined to the prediction of the Messiah. Desirous of expressing the absolute ruin of Babylon, he illustrates it by a comparison with that of Sodom and Gomorrah. And, as by Babylon is to be understood also the total extirpation of Antichrist, we have thus the Prophet's unquestionable authority for connecting the mysterious conference between God and Abraham with all the highest interests of the Gospel. but, to cut off the root of every frivolous doubt—to prove that all Scripture was given for our learning—and that the Old and New Testaments are only the Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end of God's continued word; remember that our blessed Lord, in the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, and Luke, refers to the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, as the measure of that punishment which should befall the unbelieving Jews. Would the Son of God refer to that which was untrue, or even doubtful? RememberRe-
member also, that he refers to those who doubted whether he were Christ or no, to the Scriptures. To what Scriptures?—Not to the New Testament, for that was not written till after his death. To the Old? to what part of the Old?—Perhaps to all—for all the Prophets bear witness of him; but certainly to the five books of Moses—for that Prophet, and him alone, does he by name distinguish, John v.46, 47: "Had ye believed Moses ye would have believed me, for he wrote of me: but if ye believe not his writings, how shall ye believe my words?"John 5:46-47 The inference is obvious. And my heart's desire, and prayer to God for every one of you, is, that you may be saved. If ye are yourselves desirous of it, resist the first attacks of infidelity, and its evil spirit will flee for ever: but if any one of you, in carelessness or confidence, encourage or admit these attmepts, on him, in an hour when he least expects, the devil will come like a strong man armed, will enter into his heart, will spoil him of his armour wherein he trusted, and the latter end of that man will be worse than the first.
